t: 50–70% of patients have motor difficulties persisting into adulthood (Cantell M.H. et al. J Adolesc Res 2003 follow-up; Kirby A. et al. Res Dev Disabil 2010).
ADHD 50%, specific learning disorder 50%, autism spectrum 5–10%, developmental language disorder 20–30%, increased risk of anxiety and depression in adolescence.
Heritability studies limited, but family history of DCD cited as risk factor (Gaines R. et al. Phys Occup Ther Pediatr 2008).
Neurobiological — atypical patterns of functional connectivity in the cerebellum, basal ganglia, parietal cortex, and premotor areas (Zwicker J.G. et al. Eur J Paediatr Neurol 2012 fMRI review).
... disruptions in the processes of prediction and comparison preceding motor planning (Adams I.L. et al. Dev Med Child Neurol 2014).
Premature birth (<32 weeks) and low birth weight (<1500 g) — increases DCD risk 6–8 times (Edwards J. et al. Dev Med Child Neurol 2011 systematic review).
Maternal alcohol use during pregnancy (motor difficulty component in the FASD spectrum).
DCD or other neurodevelopmental disorders in family history.
Running, jumping, ball games, cycling, balance difficulty; avoidance of sports activity.
Handwriting quality poor and slow, scissors, tying strings, buttoning, fork-knife use.
delay in learning new motor skills; problems with sequence and rhythm.
Academic performance — handwriting speed, paper organization, sports participation.
Community activity — playgrounds, sports teams, hobbies.
Psychosocial outcomes — decreased self-esteem, anxiety, depressed mood, social isolation (Cairney J. et al. Dev Med Child Neurol 2013).

Early signs (1–3 years) — delay in motor milestones (sitting, standing, first steps at normal time or delayed; quality of specific movements poor). Difficulties become evident in preschool and early school age; with increasing academic demands (writing, paper tasks), the disorder becomes more prominent. In adulthood — persistent difficulties in driving, occupational demands, sports activities (50–70%).
6.1 Unified diagnostic criteria (DSM-5-TR · ICD-11 · EACD 2019 consensus points)
A. Acquisition and execution of motor skills
— significantly below the expected level when chronological age and motor learning opportunities are considered. Difficulties lead to slowness, imprecision, incoordination in performing motor skills.
significantly and persistently interferes with age-appropriate daily life activities (e.g., self-care, academic achievement, occupational activity, play and sports).
C. Onset in early developmental period.
— Not fully explained by intellectual disability, visual impairment or other neurological condition (e.g., cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, degenerative disorders).

four diagnostic criteria standardized—score below 5–15th percentile on motor skill test (Movement Assessment Battery for Children, MABC-2 or similar); daily activity impairment; onset in early developmental period; not explained by other medical conditions. Multidisciplinary assessment (pediatrician + occupational therapist or physiotherapist) recommended. Diagnosis recommended from age 5 (after motor skills stabilize); before age 5 term 'Provisional DCD' or 'motor development delay' can be used.
EACD parallel criteria; the term "dyspraxia" is not used.
Parent and teacher interview — developmental history, motor milestones, daily activity problems.
Standardized motor test—MABC-2 (Movement Assessment Battery for Children-2, age 3–16) or BOT-2 (Bruininks-Oseretsky Test of Motor Proficiency).
Functional daily activity assessment — DCDQ (Developmental Coordination Disorder Questionnaire, parent form), VABS adaptive component.
Visual test — exclusion of sensory impairment.
Neurological examination — exclusion of cerebral palsy, ataxia, neuromuscular disease.
Comorbidity screening — ADHD (Conners), specific learning disorder, autism, language disorder.
Intellectual assessment — only if suspected global developmental delay or intellectual disability.
Spasticity, hyperreflexia, persistent neurological signs; MRI typical findings.
Progressive muscle weakness; CK level elevated; family history.
Motor deficit in the context of general cognitive deficit.
Motor stereotypies, social-communicative deficit are core.
Attention deficit affects motor performance; comorbidity is frequent (~50%).
Fine motor problem dominant in writing context; general motor function is intact.
(Movement Assessment Battery for Children-2, ages 3–16) — gold standard; manual dexterity, ball skills, balance subtests.
(Bruininks-Oseretsky Test of Motor Proficiency, ages 4–21) — broad motor profile.
(Developmental Coordination Disorder Questionnaire, ages 5–15, parent form) — functional screening.
(Concise Assessment Method for Children's Handwriting) — handwriting quality.
No routine indication. Based on specific clinical suspicion:
Thyroid function — in some hypothyroid cases motor slowing.
Genetic research — if syndromic features present.
Brain MRI — only if focal neurological signs, ataxia, stable deficits (to rule out cerebellar/cerebral pathology).
EMG/nerve conduction study — suspected neuromuscular disease.
Visual test — baseline indicators in all cases.
— Demonstrates superior effect compared to bottom-up sensorimotor approaches (Smits-Engelsman B.C. et al. Dev Med Child Neurol 2013 meta-analysis).
CO-OP (Cognitive Orientation to daily Occupational Performance)
Evidence-based approaches recommended as first-line at EACD 2019.
— daily activity goals chosen by patient and family (e.g., bicycle riding, dressing, handwriting).
